item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now this is different. Like how the agent ended up falling in love not falling under the influence. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That image really sells it, and I love the fake out with the “possible psychic manipulation” being simple infatuation

+1

item.title: Re: ?
item.content:encoded:
Looks like the page was vandalized. I changed it back.

EDIT: Not vandalism, turns out, just a glitch from some formatting work.
I'm probably missing something, but why are "object" and "neutralized" misspelled?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the image about?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is… you get articles like this from time to time, where the Foundation appears to be missing things that are blindingly obvious to the reader. What sets this apart, however, is that it's exactly the kind of situation where an institution like the Foundation would be idiotic- a situation where the alternative to stupidity is acknowledging that you've done something evil.

Which means that it's also a better-than-average example of 'the Foundation does something evil'; you can see the logic behind the decisions they made, as well as after-the-fact excuses for when it went bad. Rather than it just being openly amoral, the bureaucracy is trying to cover its own ass and justify itself.

+1

item.title: Image Credit
item.content:encoded: Image is an original work by the author

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it!

It's a short article, but it works. You did a good job of creating a character in such a short article; I think it could of used a bit more characterization, but I think what's there is enough.

+1

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: exaqt, I've cleared the tags. If you're not going to add all the tags for an article, it's best to just leave the page untagged and wait for an experienced tagger to take care of it. We have a team of people on the site who tag articles, and they search for pages that have no tags. Adding only a few tags prevents the team from finding the article, so if you're unfamiliar with the tags, it's totally fine to wait for them to take care of it.

item.title: Tags
item.content:encoded: Felt the need for tags, so I added euclid, neutralized and humanoid

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah but it feels like the whole psychological control thing was more of a fabrication to cover up what actually happened, so they would disavow someone for choosing to hide an anomaly, but also try to cover up that any… 'fraternization?' happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not so sure. I don't think they would disavow an agent for coming under the influence of suspected mind control, but only if it turned out to actually be by choice (as is implied later).

Cutting off your employees for being unwillingly effected by anomalies seems like a really good way to lose the faith of those employees.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Simple, but effective IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh… no… poor guy… though of course, this is exactly how the Foundation would operate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a quick article with a nice twist ending. Much ado is made about the SCP's capacity for mind control, but at the end it's implied that the researcher may have simply fallen in love with him. There's still a nice layer of ambiguity to the situation, which I love.

It's a very minimalist piece, though, so it's probably not to everyone's taste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your feedback! This is indeed meant to be a quick character piece, a little vignette about star-crossed lovers torn apart by the Foundation. Part of the challenge I set myself was to write the entire article in a purely impersonal tone, while giving a glimpse into a very personal tragedy. It's entirely possible that my ambitions were a bit too lofty here for that to come across. If this article doesn't make it, hopefully I'll have learned a few things for my next one!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think I get this one

I don't get enough of an attachment to the character to feel sad about their death, and if the ending feel abrupt in an unsatisfying way rather than a shocking way. Plus, I don't really feel too much for the MTF Agent that sacrificed themself for the sake of the skip, so if this was supposed to be a character piece, it's not really my thing.

If this was supposed to be something other than a character piece, I'm completely oblivious to what it was supposed to convey

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really, really good. Normally I'm not a fan of the term 'reality bender' and quantification of their abilities, but I think it suits the narrative purpose of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In this scp, I'm using "class-I reality bender" to mean "extremely limited." I decided to give it a numerical designation to emphasize that it's a very low-level ability, right off the bat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but I'm not sure if I'd classify him as a reality bender. He certainly has an anomalous ability, but I don't think it counts as bending reality.
